annotations.text: A large study involving South African adolescents suggests that 13.8% of 19-year-old women tested positive for HIV, and more than 30% tested positive by the age of 21 years.
The presence of a genital ulcer means that this patient has a high likelihood of HIV infection.
If a specimen contains HIV antibodies, the antibodies will bind to the test antigens and trigger the detection system to generate a positive result.

The acute retroviral syndrome occurs 2 to 3 weeks after infection, usually coinciding with the onset of viremia, and is accompanied by several nonspecific symptoms, including fatigue, pharyngitis, weight loss, myalgias, and headache.
The cause of the ulcer may be predicted on the basis of local epidemiologic trends.
Ruling out acute HIV infection should be the first step.
In some South African provinces, more than 75% of patients who have genital ulcers and attend a primary care clinic are found to be HIV-positive.
This patient and possibly her partner had an ulcerative sexually transmitted infection that probably facilitated transmission of the virus.
This combination of test results is diagnostic of acute HIV infection
the higher the partner's viral load, the greater the probability of male-to-female and female-to-male HIV transmission.
Treatment of the patient's recently acquired HIV infection is not so easy.

Currently, guidelines for most developed countries advocate treating asymptomatic HIV infection in patients who have a CD4+ T-cell count below 500 cells per cubic millimeter, with many suggesting treatment at higher CD4+ T-cell counts.
the patient requires intensive counseling regarding safe-sex practices.
